text: The video shows the median umbilical ligament, also known as the urachus, and the medial umbilical ligament, which is the obliterated umbilical artery. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible, forming the lateral umbilical ligament, which is not well formed.

The surgeon identifies the anatomical structures forming the triangle of doom, with the vas deferens on the left and the testicular vessels on the right. The indirect inguinal ring is visible, through which the vas deferens and vessels traverse.
A small cord-like structure becomes visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon highlights the presence of nerves in the surgical field, visible within the anatomical structures being dissected.
The surgeon identifies the lateral triangle of pain, an anatomical area bordered by the iliopubic tract and the testicular vessels, visible in the surgical field.
A finger points to the area where the dissection should begin, indicating the starting point for the surgical procedure.
The camera focuses on the area adjacent to the anterior superior iliac spine, highlighting the anatomical landmarks relevant to the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum and gradually progresses towards the medial side, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon begins the incision from the lateral portion, where a significant amount of fat is visible, facilitating easier separation of tissue planes.

The surgeon identifies the hernial defect, which is a direct hernia located medial to the inferior epigastric vessel. The peritoneal incision is made around the defect, ensuring it is at least four centimeters in length.
The surgeon positions the mesh superior to the hernial defect, ensuring adequate overlap to prevent complications during the closure of the peritoneal incision.

The surgeon applies traction with the assisting hand, which opens up the tissue planes effectively due to carbon dioxide dissection. The cobweb spaces are visible, guiding the entry into the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon applies repeated traction while carefully navigating towards the midline, pushing the vessels away to avoid injuring the nearby inferior epigastric vessels. The peritoneum is manipulated to prevent buttonhole defects and maintain the correct plane without entering the abdominal wall musculature.

The surgeon ensures hemostasis to prevent bleeding and maintain a clear surgical field. The dissection is expanded medially, approaching the medial umbilical ligament, with vessels pushed aside for better visualization and development of tissue planes.
